food_subgroup: Chilled fruit dessert with tapioca pearls and coconut milk

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for mango sago. FOODON_03301710 (dessert food product) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists specifically for mango sago. A00FL (composite food — sweet dessert) is the closest defensible parent. FoodEx2 browser consulted; no exact match found.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for mango sago or a sufficiently close parent dessert concept that would be defensible without fabrication.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Mango Sago is approximately 300 g (one bowl), as typically served in Singaporean Chinese dessert restaurants and dim sum establishments.

nutrient_source.title: Mango Sago — component-based estimation using USDA FoodData Central entries for mango (raw), tapioca pearls (cooked), and coconut milk, weighted by standard recipe proportions
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (Foundation Foods and SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for mango sago as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central using a standard recipe ratio of approximately 40% mango pulp (FDC ID 169910), 30% cooked tapioca pearls (FDC ID 169717), 25% coconut milk (FDC ID 170172), and 5% added sugar, weighted to a per-100g basis. HPB Singapore Foodo was checked; no direct entry for mango sago was found. FAO/INFOODS SEA tables were also consulted; no direct entry found. USDA component estimation is the highest-quality defensible approach available.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; tapioca/sago GI reference and mango GI reference used as component analogues
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for mango sago as a composite dish in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI is estimated as a weighted average of component GI values from approved sources: mango (GI ~51, University of Sydney GI Database), cooked tapioca/sago pearls (GI ~70 for plain tapioca, but coconut milk and mango lower the effective GI of the dish), and coconut milk (GI ~41). Weighted composite GI estimated at approximately 48 (low), consistent with the fat and protein content of coconut milk blunting the glycemic response of the tapioca starch. Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (48 × 18.5 × 3) / 100 = 26.6 g. This is an analogous estimate; direct measurement is recommended for clinical use.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content (~12 mg per 100 g); potassium from mango may support blood pressure regulation
hypertension.guidance: Mango sago is low in sodium (~36 mg per 300 g serving) and contains potassium from mango pulp, making it a generally acceptable occasional dessert for individuals managing hypertension, though added sugar content should be monitored.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (~26.6 per serving) driven by tapioca starch and mango sugars; coconut milk fat partially blunts glycemic response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume mango sago in small portions due to its moderate glycemic load of approximately 26.6 per standard 300 g serving; the fat from coconut milk partially attenuates the glycemic response, but the high sugar and starch content from mango and tapioca pearls warrants careful portion control and blood glucose monitoring.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from coconut milk (~3.3 g per 100 g); cholesterol-free; mango provides vitamin C and antioxidants
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Mango sago contains moderate saturated fat (~9.9 g per 300 g serving) from coconut milk, which may raise LDL cholesterol with frequent consumption; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size and frequency, while benefiting from the antioxidant and vitamin C content of mango.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mango Sago (芒果西米露, Máng Guǒ Xī Mǐ Lù) is a popular Chinese-origin chilled dessert widely served in Hong Kong, Singapore, and across Southeast Asia. It consists of fresh or pureed ripe mango combined with cooked tapioca pearls (sago), coconut milk, and typically a small amount of sugar, served cold. The dessert has a creamy, lightly sweet flavour with a tropical mango aroma, and the tapioca pearls provide a characteristic chewy, bouncy texture contrast against the smooth coconut-mango base. In Singapore, it is commonly found in Chinese dessert shops and dim sum restaurants. Nutritionally, mango sago provides moderate energy (~112 kcal per 100 g) primarily from carbohydrates (tapioca starch and mango sugars) and fat (coconut milk saturated fat). It is a source of vitamin C and vitamin A from mango, and is naturally cholesterol-free, gluten-free, dairy-free, and suitable for vegetarian and vegan diets. Clinically, the dish carries a moderate glycemic load per serving (~26.6) due to tapioca starch and mango sugars, and its saturated fat content from coconut milk is relevant for cardiovascular risk management. Portion control is advised for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes or cardiovascular disease.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry for mango sago as a composite dish was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA tables. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data using standard recipe proportions. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database component values. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are high quality and the recipe proportions are well-established for this dish.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-based estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central (Foundation Foods / SR Legacy) using a standard recipe ratio of 40% mango pulp, 30% cooked tapioca pearls, 25% coconut milk, and 5% added sugar. Direct laboratory analysis of this dish is recommended for high-confidence clinical use. 2. GI of 48 is an analogous estimate weighted from mango (GI ~51) and tapioca/sago (GI ~70 plain, reduced by coconut milk fat) component values from the University of Sydney GI Database; direct GI measurement of the composite dish is not available in approved sources. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero as this dish contains no hydrogenated fats; all ingredients are naturally trans-fat-free. 4. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero; coconut milk contains no dietary cholesterol and mango and tapioca are cholesterol-free. 5. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero; this is a plant-based dessert with no animal-derived ingredients. 6. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero; no vitamin D-containing ingredients are present. 7. monounsaturated_fat_g (0.2), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.1), potassium_mg (112.0), calcium_mg (10.0), iron_mg (0.4), vitamin_c_mg (14.0), vitamin_a_mcg_rae (28.0), vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg (0.03), vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg (0.02), vitamin_b3_niacin_mg (0.3), vitamin_b6_mg (0.08), folate_mcg_dfe (12.0), vitamin_e_mg (0.5), phosphorus_mg (22.0), magnesium_mg (12.0), zinc_mg (0.1), selenium_mcg (0.5), water_g (74.5) are all estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data. 8. FoodOn and FoodEx2 codes are closest-parent matches; no exact ontology code exists for mango sago. SNOMED CT has no suitable code and is set to null. 9. Serving size of 300 g is based on typical restaurant portion size for this dessert in Singapore. 10. Price data from field survey data (SGD 11.00 at Loong Dim Sum — North Canal Road) is preserved from the input record for reference but is not used in nutritional calculations.
